data.title: NLR: It's Not Just About Artificial Intelligence
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I reiterate my recommendation to buy the VanEck Uranium and Nuclear ETF (NLR). This article is a continuation of my introductory coverage article published on December 7, 2024 with the same recommendation.

President Trump has signed executive orders that directly impact the uranium and nuclear energy sector. Let's take a look at these measures, and I'll show you how the demand for artificial intelligence is just a catalyst for prices, which have long been affected by the imbalance between supply and demand.

VanEck Uranium and Nuclear ETF

The VanEck Uranium and Nuclear ETF tracks the MVIS®Global Uranium & Nuclear Energy Index. In a single asset, the investor can gain exposure to a basket of companies involved in the uranium value chain, from mining to production.

The ETF has $1.28 billion in net assets and a management fee of 0.56%. It is worth noting that there are other peers, however, the NLR ETF has the best return in the last three years, due to the mix of holdings and the low management fee.

Remembering The Thesis

This is not the first time I have written about the VanEck Uranium and Nuclear ETF. My first article was published on December 7, 2024, and at the time, I explained the history of uranium and connected it to the thesis of increased demand for artificial intelligence, the main reason for the recent surge in prices. Well, an update is necessary due to recent events in this market.

What Events Occurred?

President Trump signed executive orders last week to rapidly expand the United States’ nuclear power capacity. The goal is to quadruple US nuclear capacity from 100 GW to 400 GW by 2050. The executive orders signed included:

  • Federal funding and guarantees to support the construction of 10 new reactors by 2030, and to modernize existing ones.
  • Development and deployment of small modular reactors, known by the acronym SMRs.
  • Eliminating regulatory barriers, particularly the time it takes to review an application to build a new nuclear power plant.
  • Increasing U.S. uranium production and uranium enrichment capacity.

In short, the orders aim to accelerate the approval of new reactors and nuclear power plants. In my view, these orders are not just ambitious, they contain concrete short-term actions and heat up the global uranium market.

It's Not Just About Artificial Intelligence

In this thesis on uranium and nuclear energy, there is a detail that is rarely discussed by analysts, who are focusing on how much artificial intelligence will increase the demand for energy.

This detail is very interesting. According to the International Atomic Energy Agency, the volume of uranium production has been far below demand for a long time, as we can see in the interesting graph below.

When we analyze a shorter time frame, the imbalance between supply and demand (especially in OECD countries) is even more relevant.

This is the great margin of safety in this thesis in my opinion. The imbalance between supply and demand is already old, and the demand for energy driven by artificial intelligence is just a catalyst for the thesis, which corroborates the investment recommendation. Let's now analyze the valuation of this ETF.

Valuation

As in my previous article, we will use the price/earnings metric, which weights each company in the ETF basket. Excluding outliers, we have an average of 45x earnings, and we currently have the ETF trading at 40x earnings. If there is a return to the average P/E, we have an upside of 12.5%.

The 12.5% ​​upside alone could support the buy recommendation, however, when we analyze the uranium price metric, we see that there is even more potential, since prices are about 50% below the peak made in 2024. Therefore, my recommendation to buy the VanEck Uranium and Nuclear ETF is reiterated.

I am even more confident in my recommendation when I see that Seeking Alpha's powerful Quant tools also recommend buying the VanEck Uranium and Nuclear ETF. After looking at the positive points, let's look at the risks.

Potential Threats To The Bullish Thesis

Among the risks, it will be a major challenge to expand American production without higher uranium prices. For reference, American nuclear generators import about 99% of the uranium concentrate used in 2023, mainly from Canada, Russia and Australia.

Another challenge is that the US has not built any new nuclear power reactors for some time. In the last 10 years, only two reactors have come online in Georgia, in 2023 and 2024. Previously, there has been no nuclear reactor in operation since 2016.

Finally, much of the production is in Russia, Kazakhstan, and the African continent. Thus, geopolitical conflicts can also affect the supply of uranium. The thesis requires a pragmatic view.

The Bottom Line

Many analysts like to connect the uranium and nuclear power thesis to the energy demand of artificial intelligence, and I addressed this topic in my first article. However, the executive orders signed by President Trump gave me the opportunity to look at the case from another perspective.

The supply and demand imbalance has been going on for a long time, and the demand created by AI is just the safety margin for investment in my opinion, especially with prices near $70.

Based on this analysis, I recommend buying the VanEck Uranium and Nuclear ETF. Finally, concrete measures can be taken in the sector based on executive orders, and the risk-return ratio is quite attractive in my view.

data.title: I Bought Strategy In 2022 - Why I'm Not Buying Now
data.description:
Strategy (NASDAQ:MSTR) is a self-defined "Bitcoin Treasury Company" with roughly $60 Billion worth of Bitcoin (BTC-USD), trading at around $100 Billion in market capitalization at the time of writing.

I have covered MSTR in the past, trying to understand whether the NAV premium vs. Bitcoin holdings was justified. I downgraded the company to a HOLD in February, after finding limited progress in Saylor's vision for the company.

Today, following a new quarter of earnings and more news, I ask myself a question: why should I buy $60 worth of Bitcoin for the price of $100, instead of simply buying $100 worth of Bitcoin? In other terms, is Michael Saylor's 'magic sauce' worth a 1.6X NAV premium?

Debunking Strategy's 'NAV Premium'

VanEck recently published a great piece on Seeking Alpha on Strategy. I encourage readers who are not familiar with the company to read it. In that article, they theorize how Strategy's NAV premium is a function of three factors:

  1. Market expectations about Strategy increasing its BTC per share.

  2. A "regulatory premium" connected to the fact that MSTR is easier to trade than Bitcoin and more accessible via traditional brokerages.

  3. Strategy's use of financial leverage to conduct Bitcoin-related financial transactions.

To be clear, I fully agree these three elements are the reason why MSTR trades at a premium against its mere BTC holdings. However, I believe that MSFT is currently not "fairly valued" and that these three elements are not sufficient to justify its NAV premium.

First, for what concerns an increase in BTC per share, Strategy's BTC Yield is at ~17% YTD, with a 25% 2025 target announced during Q1 earnings. This means that Bitcoin per share has increased by 17% year to date. At this rate, it would take an investor roughly 3.2 years to "recover" the premium they paid for by choosing to buy Strategy instead of Bitcoin directly today. At a 25% yield, it would take them 2.2 years.

This means that theoretically, new investors in MSTR can assume to start getting "Bitcoin dividends" (i.e. get more BTC per share against what they paid for) in 2 to 3 years from now. However, this assumes that Saylor can keep up the BTC yield figure at above 15% for that period of time. This in turn relies on the ability of MSTR to keep diluting shareholders to buy more Bitcoin (or "monetizing volatility", in Saylor's words).

MSTR can increase its BTC Yield only as long as it trades at a NAV Premium - a premium that is in turn 'justified' by their ability to increase BTC Yield. This apparent paradox is also the reason why I do not see this system as sustainable long term, nor enough of a reason per se to justify a NAV premium at all. The moment MSTR ceases to trade at a premium, its ability to dilute shareholders and buy BTC (increasing BTC per share in return) will stop.

The second reason that could justify a NAV premium is the "regulatory premium". I find this narrative outdated, as today there are many ETFs that can be used to buy Bitcoin directly. It is true that in some specific use cases (specific retirement accounts not allowing for BTC ETFs), MSTR can still provide BTC exposure. But I think overall the maturity of the sector, especially under the current US administration, makes Strategy fundamentally unattractive as a simple Bitcoin proxy.

The third reason justifying a NAV premium - Strategy's ability to financial engineer Bitcoin products - is in my view the real point of discussion. This is what I will cover next, in more detail.

MSTR: From a 'Bitcoin Development' to a 'Bitcoin Treasury' Company

Strategy rebranded in February 2025 as a "Bitcoin Treasury Company". To be honest, I prefer what Strategy used to call itself until before the rebranding: a "Bitcoin Development Company".

I think the latter definition implied how Strategy would actively try to develop Bitcoin-related financial solutions, and participate in the evolution of the Bitcoin blockchain as one of the major single holders of Bitcoin.

In contrast, I think Strategy has blurred its mission following the rebranding. It now merges its Bitcoin "HODL" strategy with its legacy software business, calling out how they "provide cloud-native, AI-powered enterprise software [...] to explore innovation in Bitcoin application" (see image below, taken from Strategy's "About Us" section).

I do not know exactly what this claim means, nor I am sure whether Strategy's legacy software business has any connection with their Bitcoin Treasury. When it comes to what exact Bitcoin-related activity Strategy is pursuing, for the last five months the company has done what follows:

  • Rebranded and revamped their corporate website, including a home page Dashboard calling out key BTC and fiat company figures.

  • Launched an e-commerce sub-website, where users can buy Strategy and Bitcoin themed gadgets.

  • Offered $2 Billion in Convertible Notes and proposed a $250 Million preferred stock offering - which effectively would allow third parties to join Strategy's system of diluting existing shareholders in fiat terms.

Personally, I do not see any "AI-powered" Bitcoin applications in what was done so far this year.

The reality, as I see it, is that Strategy's legacy software is a business that generated just about $110 Million in Q1, decreasing 3.6% per year. While this business is highly profitable, at a ~69% margin, it can hardly justify the company's generous valuation. A declining business with about $770 Million in yearly gross profit is minuscule when compared to Strategy's $40 Billion premium over its Bitcoin holdings. I would not evaluate Strategy's software business at more than $5 Billion, even with generous growth assumptions that have not materialized yet.

In this regard, I would prefer to see Strategy completely detaching itself from its legacy software business - potentially spinning it off in a new company and buying Bitcoin with the proceeds.

Strategy, in my view, would benefit by focusing on issuing new financial products linked to Bitcoin that could justify its NAV premium. These may include offering margin loans on Bitcoins, providing consultancy services to companies that are considering adopting Bitcoin and hiring developers to actively participate in the long-term development of the BTC blockchain.

That was my original bull thesis for MSTR, which I covered on Seeking Alpha in May last year. Since then, Strategy has failed to impress me in terms of how it is developing. I don't see it going in the direction of being a "Bitcoin bank", but rather a business with an unclear focus on legacy software and simply holding Bitcoin. That's why I keep rating it a HOLD for now, waiting to see more developments from its leadership.

How I Traded Strategy in 2022

In my thesis so far, I have reasoned in Bitcoin, not in figures expressed in fiat currency. That's the approach that in my view makes the most sense with MSTR, given the company is fully invested in its Bitcoin strategy. Investors should in theory not care at all about fiat figures, as they are getting diluted in exchange for an increasing BTC per share figure.

However, I think the elephant in the room with Strategy is that investors are primarily driven by the idea that the company will overperform Bitcoin and provide them outsized returns in fiat terms in the short run. In other terms, the market sees MSTR as a leveraged BTC bet.

I believe this is the fundamental reason why Strategy's system of diluting shareholders is working so far. In a BTC bull market, euphoria and greed can keep Strategy's system of diluting shareholders to increase BTC yield running. Should Bitcoin correct and enter a bear market, I think Strategy would need a new, more solid rationale to keep justifying a NAV premium (and keep increasing its BTC holdings).

The problem is that Strategy has historically provided outsized returns to shareholders precisely when bought during BTC bear markets. That's what I did in 2022, opening a position in MSTR when the company was trading at a NAV discount of between 0.6X and 0.9X its BTC holdings. I converted most of my MSTR position to Bitcoin in early 2025, when I also downgraded the company from BUY to HOLD on Seeking Alpha.

I think buying today is a far riskier proposition, given investors are buying at a premium that may or may not close in 2 to 4 years, assuming a BTC bear market does not manifest before.

Risks to My Thesis

The main risk with my "HOLD" investment thesis is missing out on outsized returns compared to Bitcoin. While Strategy has lagged relative to Bitcoin in the last 6 months, it is still vastly overperforming Bitcoin in all longer time frames.

Strategy could overperform Bitcoin as a result of two elements. First, should the Bitcoin bull run continue, this may attract more investors into MSTR (the "greed" effect I describe before). This would fuel Strategy's proven system, where Saylor dilutes shareholders in fiat terms to have them gain in BTC terms (albeit starting at a significant premium for the BTC they paid for).

The second element concerns Saylor's dilution in fiat terms. Strategy's shares outstanding have increased significantly in the past two years, with a ~48% growth in Q1 2025 alone, as shown by the chart below.

By pausing shares dilution, Strategy can effectively have shareholders benefit in fiat terms. To be clear, I do not expect that to happen in the case of a BTC bull run. Saylor has been very clear about focusing on BTC Yield, and I find it unlikely Strategy will limit shareholders' dilution in a bull market. I would, however, expect them to stop dilution in case the NAV Premium gets closer to 1X. The less premium, the less BTC Yield MSTR can achieve by diluting shareholders.

Conclusion

In my opinion, Michael Saylor has yet to prove why his company is worthy of a NAV premium against BTC holding in the long run.

Behind many of the fancy words the company uses, such as "monetizing volatility" or "leverage 35+ years of software expertise to explore innovation in Bitcoin applications", the company runs a fairly simple system based on retail interest:

  • Investors buy MSTR because they believe it will overperform BTC even starting at a NAV premium, fueled by greed during a BTC bull market.

  • Strategy dilutes investors to buy more Bitcoin, increasing its BTC yield (i.e., how much BTC shareholders are "entitled to" for each of their shares).

  • An increase in BTC yield, in turn, fuels the investment narrative of MSTR as a "Bitcoin Treasury Company".

I think this system can only be sustainable long term if Strategy proves there are fundamentals on why a NAV premium is justified. In other terms, I do not see it sustainable just based on an increasing BTC yield narrative.

Strategy could, for example, launch financial products linked to Bitcoin or consultancy services tied to adopting Bitcoin as a corporate treasury holding. While Strategy has shown some progress in that direction, I do not see any significant progress yet.

That's why I rate the company a HOLD. If you own a small amount of MSTR stock with a safe margin, I recommend holding it as a bet. Personally, I still have faith in Michael Saylor and am eager to see if Strategy will offer Bitcoin services to bridge traditional finance and Bitcoin. However, I would rather buy more Bitcoin than trusting

data.title: China's Biotech Revolution Rolls Forward To The Global Frontiers Of Medical Therapeutics
data.description:
By Justin Leverenz, Chief Investment Officer, Developing Markets Equities

China is unique among developing markets, not only for the sheer size of its economy1, but for its place at the forefront of global innovation in biotechnology. As long-term investors in China’s biotechnology innovation space, we are enormously encouraged by the dynamic progress of these leading Chinese companies. How did China’s biotech industry grow so big and so fast — and how much further can it grow?

A decade of growth and innovation

The story started in 2015, when Chinese regulators eliminated the need for big upfront capital expenditures to build manufacturing plants, which allowed biotech startups to accelerate their development.

Then in 2017, China officially joined the International Council for Harmonisation of Technical Requirements for Pharmaceuticals for Human Use (ICH), which allowed them to match global drug regulatory system and standards. ICH standards, along with China’s large patient pool, have enabled much faster patient recruitment for trials at a lower cost. Hence, pipeline drug assets can much more rapidly gain proof of concept data to help overcome any biological/clinical risks.2

China’s large talent pool has been the critical link between these factors and its rapid move to leadership position in the biotech innovation space.

To put this in perspective, in the past five years China has leaped from fast follower to global pioneer in drug discovery and development. It leap-frogged research and development of conventional chemical and biologic drugs into the forefront of new complex modalities, which can potentially be more efficacious against devastating diseases such as cancer.

Clinical and commercial leadership

Today, Chinese leaders in biotechnology innovation include both conventional biotech or pharma drug companies and CRDMO (Contract Research Development and Manufacturing Organization) companies. The CRDMO players partner with biotech/biopharma companies across all stages of drug research and development and commercialization such as discovering a new drug molecule, designing the manufacturing steps of these molecules, and manufacturing drug molecules for trials or commercialization post approvals.

Chinese companies have established global clinical and commercial leadership in hematology oncology therapeutics, including Carvykti (a treatment from Legend Biotech in partnership with Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) for adult patients who have cancer of the bone marrow called multiple myeloma) and Brukinsa (a treatment from BeiGene for non-Hodgkins lymphoma). As a result, China has become a rich hunting ground for global pharma and biotech leaders looking to enrich their clinical pipelines, including AstraZeneca (AZN), Merck (MRK), GlaxoSmithKline, Johnson & Johnson, BioNtech (BNTX), and more.3 China went from contributing 4% of global biotech out-licensing deals to global pharma companies in 2019-2020 to 12% in 2023-2024, valued at $48 billion.4

The key to unlocking further value creation

In our opinion, China’s biotech sector is still in its early stages. While it’s already a top source of biopharmaceutical innovation globally, the value it captures is limited because it has yet to tap into a key area of value creation – late-stage global clinical development and commercialization. Evidence suggests that this could be the next stage in China’s emergence on the world biotech scene.

We believe that, over time, China biotech revenues and profits are likely to see a step-function expansion through milestone and royalty revenues tied to clinical development progress and commercialization. Much of the biotech hunt for China is about probability adjusting for such milestones/royalties by closely following clinical and commercial progress of assets in the China biotech and CDRMO space.

The CDRMO opportunity is, in our view, enormous and relatively low risk. These are service businesses with stable and growing cash flows and large optionality associated with intellectual property licensing.

We believe that Chinese companies have established large competitive advantages in costs and speed of discovery and development. For example:

  • Wuxi Biologics - the largest biologics CRDMO in China - was involved in 68% of total biopharma licencing collaboration contracts signed by multinational companies.5
  • Wuxi Apptec (OTCPK:WUXAY), a CDRMO leader in research and discovery of new chemical entities, has led the development and commercial manufacturing of two of the most recent obesity blockbusters from Eli Lilly (LLY).
  • WuXi XDC, a global leader in bioconjugates CRDMO platform, has rapidly gained global leadership status for the much more complicated discovery and development manufacturing of antibody drug conjugates.

The Invesco Developing Markets Fund is focused on structural growth companies with durable advantages and real options, which has led us to several interesting investments in health care companies in China and beyond.

As we said at the outset, China is now at the forefront of global innovation. It is still early days, and we are excited to have front row seats to what could be some of the most important – and life-changing – drug discoveries in history.

Footnotes

  • 1China represents 31% of the MSCI Emerging Market Index and 20% of global gross domestic product. Source: Index Weight – MSCI as of 3/31/2025. GDP based on Purchasing Power Parity – International Monetary Fund as of 12/31/2024.
  • 2Source: International Council for Harmonisation of Technical Requirements for Pharmaceuticals for Human Use (ICH) 2017.
  • 3Source: AstraZeneca, Wuxi Biologics, Wuxi Apptec, Legend, BioNtech represented 1.5%, 0.9%, 0.7%, 0.6%, and 0.3% of the Invesco Developing Markets Fund respectively as of 3/31/2025. The fund has no exposure to Johnson and Johnson, Merck, GlaxoSmithKline, BeiGene or Eli Lilly. Holdings are subject to change and are not buy/sell recommendations.
  • 4Source: Generics and Biosimilars Initiative as of 01/21/2025.
  • 5Source: Wuxi Biologics, company internal analysis as of 01/15/2025.

Important Information

NA4529016

Photographer credit: Yuguang Zhang / Getty

All investing involves risk, including the risk of loss.

Past performance does not guarantee future results.

Investments cannot be made directly in an index.

This does not constitute a recommendation of any investment strategy or product for a particular investor. Investors should consult a financial professional before making any investment decisions.

The risks of investing in securities of foreign issuers, including emerging market issuers, can include fluctuations in foreign currencies, political and economic instability, and foreign taxation issues.

The MSCI Emerging Markets Index captures large- and mid-cap representation across 26 Emerging Markets (EM) countries. With 1,198 constituents, the index covers approximately 85% of the free-float-adjusted market capitalization in each country.

In general, stock values fluctuate, sometimes widely, in response to activities specific to the company as well as general market, economic and political conditions.

Growth stocks tend to be more sensitive to changes in their earnings and can be more volatile. The risks of investing in securities of foreign issuers, including emerging market issuers, can include fluctuations in foreign currencies, political and economic instability, and foreign taxation issues.

The health care industry is subject to risks relating to government regulation, obsolescence caused by scientific advances and technological innovations.

Investments focused in a particular industry, such as pharmaceuticals and biotechnology, are subject to greater risk, and are more greatly impacted by market volatility, than more diversified investments.

Investing in securities of Chinese companies involves additional risks, including, but not limited to: the economy of China differs, often unfavorably, from the U.S. economy in such respects as structure, general development, government involvement, wealth distribution, rate of inflation, growth rate, allocation of resources and capital reinvestment, among others; the central government has historically exercised substantial control over virtually every sector of the Chinese economy through administrative regulation and/or state ownership; and actions of the Chinese central and local government authorities continue to have a substantial effect on economic conditions in China.

The investment techniques and risk analysis used by the portfolio managers may not produce the desired results.

The opinions referenced above are those of the author as of May 29, 2025. These comments should not be construed as recommendations but as an illustration of broader themes. Forward-looking statements are not guarantees of future results. They involve risks, uncertainties, and assumptions; there can be no assurance that actual results will not differ materially from expectations.

data.title: American Healthcare REIT's Growth Story Is Still Young
data.description:
Senior housing represents one of the most exciting growth trends in the commercial real estate asset class.

Right now, we are still in the early stages of this growth trend. The population of seniors (aged 80+) in the United States is projected to soar in the coming decades, virtually assuring increasing demand for these facilities. And unlike the pre-pandemic years, construction costs have become prohibitively expensive for new development in many cases, which is currently suppressing construction starts.

That combination of robust demand and limited supply is a powerful one that contains the makings of a superb long-term investment.

That's where American Healthcare REIT (NYSE:AHR) (OTCPK:AHTR) comes in.

It is a diversified healthcare real estate investment trust with a focus on high-quality senior housing facilities. A minority of its portfolio is dedicated to outpatient medical buildings and triple-net leased senior housing, but it is deemphasizing those in order to concentrate on higher-growth operating senior housing.

The biggest question I asked myself when I realized the attractiveness of AHR's growth prospects recently was, "Did I miss my chance to buy?"

After an astounding 134% rally over the last year...

...AHR is now valued at 21.5x normalized funds from operations ("NFFO") and about 25x adjusted FFO ("AFFO"), which is basically NFFO minus maintenance capex.

That sounds expensive... until you compare it to AHR's growth outlook, which is phenomenal. Analysts estimate 27.5% NFFO per share growth this year, followed by 10-12% in both 2026 and 2027.

Compare that to senior housing giant Welltower (WELL), arguably AHR's closest peer in the REIT space, which is currently valued at 30.3x FFO and ~34x AFFO.

Now, WELL has a scale advantage, being over 10x larger than AHR, as well as a slightly lower-leveraged balance sheet, so it probably deserves a premium valuation. But analysts are not forecasting meaningfully faster growth for WELL than for AHR, which leads me to believe that either WELL is overvalued, AHR is undervalued, or a bit of both.

WELL's price to estimated 2027 FFO is almost 24x, while AHR's price to estimated 2027 FFO sits at 17.3x.

In my view, WELL looks a little too richly valued, while AHR looks too cheaply valued.

Just how much upside does it have to fair value? I don't know. I think it's a fool's game to guess at something like that.

But even if AHR enjoys no further multiple expansion from here, the REIT still looks likely to generate double-digit total annual returns going forward, based on its 2.9% dividend yield and growth sustainably in the high single-digits to low double-digits.

As such, I view AHR as an attractive buy for long-term investors. I recently bought a small position.

What Makes AHR A High-Quality Compounder

As stated in the introduction, AHR's healthcare portfolio is diversified, but the REIT is becoming more and more concentrated in operating senior housing properties over time.

By "operating," I am referring to two different types of assets:

  1. Integrated Senior Health Campuses ("ISHC"), which offer both assisted living and skilled nursing so that residents can age in place.
  2. Senior Housing Operating Properties ("SHOP"), which are operated by third-party operators for a management fee while giving the landlord significant exposure to changes in revenue, expenses, and NOI.

Management are focused on growing only their ISHC and SHOP segments, while opportunistically disposing of non-core outpatient medical buildings and triple-net leased properties over time.

I like this because ISHC and SHOP offer by far the most long-term organic growth potential while still being attractively priced in the 7-8% cap rate range.

To illustrate this, consider that Q1 2025 total portfolio same-store NOI growth came in at 15.1%, split across its four property types:

  • ISHC: 19.8% (62% of portfolio-wide SSNOI)
  • Outpatient Medical: 2.0% (19% of SSNOI)
  • SHOP: 30.7% (11% of SSNOI)
  • Triple-Net Leases: -1.4% (8% of SSNOI)

Total portfolio SSNOI growth guidance for 2025 is 9-13%, so management does not expect Q1's ~15% SSNOI growth rate to be sustained indefinitely. But 9-13% SSNOI growth is still remarkably strong organic growth!

Turning to portfolio geography, I'll note that there's some concentration risk, as virtually all ISHC (Trilogy) properties are located in the Midwestern states of Indiana, Kentucky, and Ohio.

The SHOP portfolio, on the other hand, is well diversified across the country with 7 different regional operators.

Management emphasize that they seek to own facilities and partner with operators that focus on providing high-quality care, insisting that this is a critical component of their fundamental success in the long run.

For example, ISHC facilities boast high CMS quality scores, which are administered by the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services. The average building age of ISHC properties is slightly over 10 years, while the bed mix is split between senior housing (43% of beds) and skilled nursing (57% of beds).

The model of ISHC is similar to SHOP in that all of these facilities are managed by Trilogy Management Services, with whom AHR has been closely partnered for some time now.

Unlike continuing care retirement communities ("CCRC"), in which new residents typically pay an entrance fee and start out on the independent living side, ISHC communities are focused solely on residents that require varying levels of care, from assisted living to skilled nursing.

Likewise, in its SHOP portfolio, a little over 8 in 10 of beds are in assisted living facilities.

As such, AHR's senior housing portfolio is mostly geared toward need-based rather than lifestyle-based facilities. This could insulate the portfolio somewhat from cyclical economic swings.

On the other hand, it does expose AHR to more funding from government programs like Medicare and Medicaid, which is an entirely different risk. For example, the Congressional Budget Office recently estimated that 11 million people, including 7.8 million Medicaid recipients, would lose insurance coverage if the current version of the congressional budget bill became law.

However, these coverage losses are entirely focused on the non-elderly adult population, and cuts to Medicaid for senior care do not look likely at this point.

Turning to demographic tailwinds, it is truly hard to overestimate the size of the "silver tsunami" heading our way.

Notice in the image below that the 80+ population is expected to grow by over 700,000 people per year through at least 2030, while the current level of construction starts comes nowhere near that. Since the dark days of the COVID-19 pandemic, in which senior housing occupancy fell sharply, the industry has rebounded mightily, and absorption has far outpaced supply growth.

AHR's management cite third-party data from the National Investment Center for Seniors Housing, estimating that at the current pace of development, there will be a ~$275 billion shortfall of senior housing units by 2030.

That implies continued growth in occupancy, rent rates, and NOI across AHR's senior housing portfolio.

Indeed, recent research from NIC projects that, based on current trends, senior housing occupancy should exceed their prior peak levels by next year -- 2026.

Quoting the NIC report:

Occupied unit levels continue to grow steadily, with no signs of a demand cliff.

I repeat: "No signs of a demand cliff."

As I said before, this combination of strong demand and limited supply is a recipe for sustained cash flow growth.

Balance Sheet

The best REITs combine high-quality portfolios with similarly high-quality balance sheets, a combo that tends to result in a strong (read: low) cost of capital.

That, I believe, is a fair characterization of AHR.

The balance sheet is modestly leveraged at a net debt to EBITDA of 4.5x, and the REIT's debt is termed out well, with some loans not maturing until the 2050s. Hence, the 12.5-year weighted average debt term.

There is a sizable debt maturity wall (maybe hurdle would be a better word?) coming in 2027, but after that, annual maturities look very manageable.

Management are targeting investment grade credit ratings and appear to be well on their way to earning them, although I can't predict how long that will take.

Looking at the debt structure differently below, we can see what those ultra-long-term loans are.

AHR's mortgage loans boast both the lowest interest rates and the longest average maturity of all its debt (excluding JV debt).

That is because AHR's ISHC segment qualifies for low-cost mortgages from the US Department of Housing And Urban Development. The REIT secured 30-year mortgages from HUD during the period of low interest rates a few years back, demonstrating a farsighted and conservative financing philosophy that I believe to be intelligent.

Also intelligent, in my view, is management's prudent use of common equity issuance via the ATM program in recent quarters.

In Q1, AHR tapped the ATM to raise $47.7 million from common equity issuance at an average share price of $30.22 (5.3% NFFO yield, or 4.6% AFFO yield). As previously stated, this capital is being used to invest in new assets at cash yields in the 7-8% range, making them immediately accretive to FFO per share.

On top of this attractively valued equity issuance, I estimate that AHR has about $27 million in annualized retained cash after the dividend.

While the NFFO payout ratio is in the mid-60% area, I estimate the AFFO payout ratio to be about 85%. The $1.00 annualized dividend has stayed flat since the REIT went public in early 2024, which makes a dividend hike likely sometime this year.

AHR has guided for $80-100 million development project investments this year. Plus, management expect $86 million of acquisitions in the first half of 2025 and has a $300 million pipeline of high-probability investment opportunities (at unknown closing dates) at cap rates in the 7-8% territory.

Between AHR's strong cost of equity and rapid organic growth, the REIT's leverage ratios should quickly come down, which in turn should result in a lower cost of debt.

I love this growth model. It's a virtuous cycle.

In fact, I wrote an entire article about this a while back, titled "The Virtuous Cycle: Why I Love 'Expensive' REITs."

While I, of course, want to buy REITs at as low a valuation as possible, the kind of REITs I want to own typically trade at a high enough equity valuation to make common stock issuance an attractive and accretive form of capital for investments.

AHR should be able to continue issuing low-cost equity for accretive growth, which in turn should push up its stock price and keep its cost of equity low.

Of course, I would've much rather bought AHR in February 2024, when the stock was much cheaper and higher-yielding. But I believe we are still early in AHR's virtuous cycle of growth.

Consider, for example, the size difference between AHR and WELL's total assets:

If AHR is able to scale up its portfolio like WELL has done over the last decade or so, then it has a long external growth runway ahead of it.

Bottom Line

If I could send an email back in time to myself in February 2024, I would tell myself to buy AHR. (Of course, if I had that power, there are several stocks I'd tell my past self to buy.)

But the past is a memory. The only thing that exists is the present moment. And at this present moment, AHR looks like a compelling long-term investment opportunity.

I believe the management team are smart capital allocators in a space with enduring and powerful demographic tailwinds. The growth in the 80+ population is projected to be faster than any time on record over the next few decades.

Combined with limited supply growth over the next several years, these demographic tailwinds result in a growing supply-demand imbalance that should be highly favorable to senior housing landlords like AHR.

Its portfolio is made up of recently constructed properties, high-quality operators, and defensive, need-based services. And the balance sheet is conservatively capitalized and low leveraged, setting the REIT up for eventual investment grade credit ratings.

Lastly, AHR is reasonably valued.

Comparing the price-to-operating cash flow multiples of AHR and WELL, we find that AHR is valued at nearly half that of WELL.

To be fair, a better and more accurate valuation metric would be price-to-AFFO. Even here, AHR is significantly cheaper than WELL.

AHR's price to estimated 2025 AFFO is ~25x, while WELL's price to estimated 2025 AFFO is ~34x.

Again, WELL probably deserves to trade at a premium, but I don't think AHR should trade at that much of a discount to its close peer.

Even if that discount persists, though, AHR should still generate strong total returns as it executes on the virtuous cycle described above.

While calling AHR a "wonderful company at a wonderful price" might be a stretch, I think it would be fair to characterize it as a "wonderful company at a fair price."

And in the long run, I believe it will make an excellent dividend growth investment.

data.title: Etsy: GMS Continues To Slide, But At Least Profits Are Firm
data.description:
With the stock market rallying against high hopes for fresh trade deals, I continue to encourage investors to emphasize individual stock picking: but caution is key. Valuation multiples are stretched for the highest-quality companies, forcing us to look downmarket at more challenged businesses.

Etsy (ETSY) is one of these names. The arts-and-crafts marketplace has been pressured by declining gross merchandise sales [GMS] for over a year, as demand soured from post-pandemic highs. The stock has been stuck in a range-bound trading pattern, though Etsy recently jumped more than 25% on the back of a well-received Q1 earnings update. The question for investors now is: will Etsy find direction moving forward?

I last upgraded Etsy to a neutral opinion back in April, when the stock was trading in the mid-$45s after having just announced the sale of its underperforming subsidiary, Reverb (a site for used musical instruments and other accessories). The company just confirmed the sale at $105 million in all-cash, valuing the business at a double-digit multiple of trailing segment EBITDA and providing a much-needed cash injection for the company. I continue to be encouraged by the company's consistent ability to improve its EBITDA margins, but without a reasonable path back to GMS growth, I remain at a neutral rating here.

To me, I see a rather mixed bag of positives and negatives for Etsy. One of the more positive recent developments that I see in the company is its acknowledgement that its sales are flagging, and as a result, the company is reworking its marketing spend to try to address these challenges.

The chart above showcases that while Etsy is slightly cutting its overall marketing spend (both in nominal dollar terms as well as a percentage of revenue), the company is mixing more heavily into performance marketing while heavily reducing its brand marketing spending. To me, this is a smart move to stimulate short-term performance: after all, Etsy's brand is already well burnished into its addressable market - and what it needs is specific triggers for customers to purchase.

At the same time, however, the company is facing a litany of challenges, including:

  • Weak consumer spending stacked on top of weak category performance. Since the heyday of the pandemic, Etsy has struggled to repeat its sudden and sharp rebound in sales, which was driven by a cluster of families moving during COVID in search of more space. Since that time, people have gradually migrated back to cities, reducing the demand for decor and home goods. When stacking this weak category performance against an increasingly tough macro situation in which consumers are pulling back on spending, we don't see a near-term path back to GMS growth.
  • Incredibly competitive marketplace. Though Etsy likes to stress that its many handmade and artisan goods are its key differentiator, these premium items may only carry weight during boom times. The company faces sharp competition from a number of other crafts and decorations companies, ranging from online superstores like Amazon (AMZN) to more upmarket retailers like Restoration Hardware (RH).
  • Ad growth may slow. Right now, the only reason Etsy is able to maintain revenue growth and margin performance is by pushing seller ads. But if GMS continues to slow and sellers don't recognize benefits from advertising on Etsy, this growth stream for the company could also wither out.

I'm encouraged by signs that Etsy's GMS performance, at the very least, isn't getting worse (and guidance implies that it may even improve slightly as we move throughout the year, though that's driven in part as well by easing prior-year comps). I don't see tremendous near-term downside for the company: but at the same time, I also see Etsy as continuing to trade in a range-bound pattern until we see tremendously different results in GMV.

For now, steer clear here and invest elsewhere.

Q1 download

Let's now go through Etsy's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q1 earnings summary is shown below:

Etsy's revenue grew at a slim 0.8% y/y pace to $651.2 million, beating Wall Street's more pessimistic expectations of $641.6 million (-0.7% y/y) by a meaningful 150bps margin.

Where we are incredibly disappointed, however, is in Etsy’s large drops in GMS. As a reminder, GMS is a measure of the total sales made on Etsy’s platform - and hence the most holistic metric to measure the health of Etsy overall. As shown in the chart below, Etsy’s GMS of $2.8 billion slipped -6.5% y/y.

The only positive read on this is the fact that overall growth improved slightly, accelerating 30bps relative to a -6.8% decline in Q4. Also, we note that FX weighed on Etsy as a headwind in Q1, as constant currency GMS growth would have been 80bps better. This headwind should turn into a tailwind in Q2 and beyond, with the dollar weakening sharply against most global currencies after Trump’s tariff announcements (thus earning Etsy more when international revenue is translated back into dollars).

The only way that Etsy is able to maintain slightly positive revenue growth is primarily via higher attach rates and strong pricing on ads. As shown in the chart below, these (alongside higher attach rates on payment processing) were the key drivers in boosting Etsy’s take rates (revenue as a percentage of GMS) by 170bps y/y.

Unfortunately, I view these as more transitory drivers that are ultimately tied to GMS performance. If platform sales are low, so will sellers’ willingness to pay to be featured on Etsy. While take rate expansion is blunting the impact of weaker sales in the short run, in the long run, GMS growth in the only sustainable path for Etsy to keep growing.

Etsy’s guidance is ambiguous, but leaves the door open to interpret some sequential improvement into next quarter: the company is indicating that GMS will decline at a similar rate to Q1, with a possibility for slightly better performance.

Here, as previously mentioned, a tweak in Etsy’s advertising playbook is one of the levers the company is deploying to try to improve performance. It’s shifting away from brand marketing to performance marketing, but it did acknowledge that its ad performance in social media is lagging - though it’s still planning on investing more into social to try to improve customer growth from this channel. Per CFO Lanny Baker’s remarks on the Q1 earnings call:

We reduced brand spend coming off last year's debut of the Etsy Marketplace's new gifting experiences, and we increased performance marketing spend year-to-year with a growing emphasis on social media channels.

Our ROIs in paid social are not as high today as what we achieve in Search and PLA. However, we're making steady gains in efficiency and Etsy Marketplace GMS from paid social increased meaningfully in the quarter. This progress enables us to underwrite greater social investment for Etsy, where we are optimistic about fueling the buyer engagement flywheel via social as Josh described earlier.”

The company notes that buyer reactivations from previously lapsed accounts remained strong, particularly in the U.S.

On profitability, Etsy's adjusted EBITDA margins improved slightly, up 30bps y/y to 26.3%, while nominal adjusted EBITDA also grew 2% y/y to $171 million. Here, slightly more expensive server costs (from implementing more ML-powered recommendation features) as well as higher headcount in the product development org were offset by efficiencies in G&A and marketing spend.

The company also notes, fortunately, that tariffs will have a minimal impact on the business, since less than 1% of the company's GMS is from U.S. buyers purchasing items made in China. Other markets also tend to source from in-country, and the vast majority of Etsy's sales are currently under the $800 de minimis exemption.

Valuation and key takeaways

One of the major advantages to Etsy from an investment perspective is that it remains cheap. As shown in the chart below, the stock trades at 2.7x forward revenue and 10.5x forward adjusted EBITDA: multiples that have compressed heavily over the past several years.

However, we have to take that price into context: this is a company that is virtually no longer growing. With Etsy now estimating that take rates will be roughly flat to Q1 levels, its overall growth in revenue and profitability will eventually track alongside GMS trends - which is currently declining in the high single digits. We note that the sale of Reverb, while presenting a welcome cash infusion, will be a headwind to GMS as well.

All in all, I don't see any compelling reasons to buy Etsy now. Stay on the sidelines here for now.

data.title: Sabesp: The Brazilian Utilities Stock Your Portfolio Desperately Needs
data.description:
Companhia de Saneamento Básico do Estado de São Paulo (SBS), or SABESP for short, is a $14 billion Brazilian water utilities company. It is the most prominent provider of basic and environmental sanitation services in the country, and serves residential, commercial, and industrial clients.

The company was privatized last year, with the São Paulo state government reducing its stake in the company from nearly 51% to barely 18%, raising nearly $2.7 billion. As such, this was the largest privatization deal in the Latin American zone in over 2 years.

Following its privatization, SABESP clearly seems to be going through a significant transformation. Broadly speaking, being run from a for-profit, private-sector mindset has fired up its growth engine, leading to sharper capital allocation, and a boost in operational efficiency.

What makes the SABESP opportunity most unique, in my point of view, is that you don't find these kinds of growth dynamics in mature markets. The heavy regulation in developed regions allows only for incremental growth at best. In a developing market such as Brazil, with strong secular trends such as urbanization, policy shifts towards universal water access and climate-consciousness, privatization unlocks a different kind of upside that I like to call catch-up growth. This represents the significant white space for infrastructure expansion, modernization, and service quality improvement which allows the company to capture massive gains over time.

What makes SBS even more of a one-of-a-kind opportunity is the fact that it is so attractively priced, despite its exceptional growth and profitability metrics.

I rate the stock as a buy.

Long-Term Growth Improving Operating Leverage

One of the most appealing aspects I find about SABESP relates to its strong operating leverage, which is all the more critical in the utilities space. While the company's adjusted net revenue climbed by less than 4%, its EBITDA actually saw a 17% YoY growth rate during the quarter, reaching BRL3 billion (or about $522 million). The improvement on this front can easily be gauged by assessing the company's trailing EBITDA margin trend over the last 5 years:

Utilities companies like SABESP are able to ensure such strong operational leverage dynamics due to the higher proportion of fixed cost structures, which allows for even a modest revenue gain to result in a sharp expansion in profit margins. This sets up SBS for strong growth and value creation over the long term for its shareholders, given the broader tailwinds it enjoys. I have identified 3 of these below:

  1. The government has been pushing for universal water and sewage coverage across the entire country, in both urban centers and rural settlements. Currently, 35 million Brazilians lack access to treated water, while over 100 million reside in dwellings that are disconnected from the country's sewage system. This creates a massive TAM for SBS over the next several years.
  2. Somewhat related to the previous point, there is a strong urbanization trend in Brazil with informal settlements increasingly being integrated into official grids. While this has been in play since at least the 1990s (following the tough policy of eradicating the country's favelas, prior to that), the trend has picked up in recent years.

These secular trends are catalyzed by developments such as the UAE investing in Brazilian infrastructure, with a particular focus on connecting favelas to urban centers through modern transport modes such as high-speed trains and subways. These moves would broadly increase the demand for SABESP's services and are likely to add high-volume consumers with long lifespans.

3. With the rising awareness of climate-related risks, utilities plays like SABESP are positioned favorably as governments (as well as investors) prioritize resilient infrastructure. With issues such as water scarcity, a higher frequency of extreme weather events, and growing urban populations, there is a greater need for strategic, long-term planning of water resources. This is something SABESP actively addresses.

SABESP's smart metering initiatives, pressure control mechanisms, and dam management effectively future-proof its business by aligning it with ESG mandates. As a result, the company could potentially unlock access to lower-cost green financing and attract institutional capital.

Increased CapEx Strengthens Moat

Another dimension to look at for SBS is its strategic CapEx spend. In its recent Q1 results, the company announced that it more than doubled its CapEx, on a YoY basis, from $118.6 million to $265 million. In fact, as a result of this trend (which has been crucial following its privatization), SABESP has not only doubled its CapEx year-over-year, but also tripled its supplier base, and is executing over 90 projects simultaneously. This is especially critical in the context of their universalization goal by 2029.

The expansion of its supplier base brings strong benefits to the company's business, such as a reduction in execution bottlenecks, risk reduction through diversification, as well as an accelerated approach to infrastructure deployment. In fact, this heavy spending on the CapEx front is very likely to deliver operational cost savings in the future, and improve margins and EBITDA sustainability. For utilities companies like SBS, this is one of the few strategic levers to boost margin in the long term.

An example is the company's focus on smart meter installations (which scaled 25% YoY) and initiatives such as waste water reduction through infrastructure modernization. These moves not only drastically minimize inefficiencies and wastages, but also deliver measurement accuracy and consumption transparency. The gains on this front were further reinforced by SABESP's crackdown against water theft, with over 7300 cases of fraud identified and addressed, bringing about a reduction in non-revenue water supply.

SBS has also highlighted that given its increased focus on these investments, it is now serving an additional 98,000 people each day, and treating 32 million liters of sewage daily. This CapEx spend, therefore, gives the company a strong social and environmental ROI, in addition to the operational gains discussed.

Valuation Assessment

SBS holds a Quant score of 4.85, which is the "strong buy" zone, and the highest among the water utilities industry. This immediately adds a strong point of attraction to the stock.

Looking further, its valuation multiples appear to be priced quite attractively, relative to the broader utilities sector, given its trailing EV/EBITDA ratio of 5.3, compared to the sector median of 12. It trades with this discount, despite seeing its EBITDA climb by 103% over the last 12 months (compared to the wider sector seeing a median growth rate of below 9%). Even on a forward basis, the company expects to grow its EBITDA by 16% over the next year, while the sector expects growth on this front to amount to 9%.

In fact, SBS does not only excel in terms of its growth metrics, but also by its high profitability. The company, with its strong CapEx approach, boasts a return on capital figure of 17.5%, while the median seen in the utilities sector is only 4%.

Together, both these metrics indicate how SBS is far more effective at generating value for its shareholders than its peers in the same space, and despite this, the stock trades at lower multiples. This discount, even where a premium would be justified, is very attractive in my opinion. I rate the stock as a buy.

Risk Assessment

One significant risk to keep watch of would be that of a potential CapEx payback lag. For a company like SABESP, which is currently following a CapEx-heavy model with long payback periods, it is likely that investments are unlikely to yield meaningful gains in revenue for several quarters, or even years. This creates a valuation lag and potential free cash flow pressure. Whether or not, or to what degree these investments result in earnings expansion is something investors should watch closely. This lack of visibility is the one risk I would be most concerned about.

Takeaway

I strongly believe that SABESP offers a one-of-a-kind opportunity to investors. Its position as a large utilities player in a developing market allows it to enjoy long-term secular growth trends, while its privatization has unlocked a massive opportunity for value creation.

Furthermore, the company's focused CapEx strategy is ensuring the steady set up of a defensible moat. All these plus points with such attractive valuation metrics make the stock's risk-reward dynamics highly compelling.

I rate SBS as a buy.

This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Best International Stock Idea investment competition, which runs through June 6. With cash prizes, this competition -- open to all analysts -- is one you don't want to miss. If you are interested in becoming an analyst and taking part in the competition, click here to find out more and submit your article today!

data.title: Spire Could Be A Good Diversifier, And Missouri Could See Data Center Development
data.description:
Spire Inc. (SR) is a regulated natural gas utility that primarily serves customers in the U.S. states of Alabama, Missouri, and Mississippi:

We can also see that Spire owns a midstream company based out of Texas that markets natural gas all over the United States, and also operates pipelines and storage facilities in Wyoming and Oklahoma. This is not unusual, as many large natural gas utilities also own midstream companies. For example, Atmos Energy (ATO) owns an intrastate natural gas transmission pipeline company in Texas. It makes sense for a natural gas utility to own a midstream company (and a natural gas marketing firm) as a way to ensure that the utility company has access to an adequate supply of natural gas to meet its needs.

As is the case with other natural gas utilities that also have a midstream arm, the company’s utility unit is much larger and far more profitable. For the six-month period that ended on March 31, 2025, the utility company had total revenue of $1.5837 billion. This compares to a much smaller $86.6 million for the natural gas marketing operation and an even smaller $71.9 million for the natural gas midstream business:

As we can see, Spire reported total consolidated operating revenue of $1.7204 billion for the six-month period, which corresponds to the first half of its 2025 fiscal year (Spire has a fiscal year running from October through September). Of that total, 92.05% came from the natural gas utility.

In addition to accounting for the largest proportion of Spire’s revenue, the natural gas utility accounts for nearly all of the company’s capital expenditures. For the first half of the 2025 fiscal year (October 1, 2024, through March 31, 2025), Spire had total capital expenditures of $479 million. Of that amount, $395 million was spent on the company’s utility infrastructure. Only $84 million was spent on the midstream business segment. Furthermore, Spire expects to have total capital expenditures of $840 million. Of this amount, only $95 million will be invested in the midstream operation:

Thus, no matter how we choose to look at it, Spire’s natural gas utility business is by far the largest proportion of its operations. As such, we should largely consider this company to be a utility. The midstream operation is too small to have a significant impact on the company’s business or stock performance.

Recent Performance Update

The last time that we discussed Spire was on December 24, 2024. The market has exhibited some volatility since that time, which is at least partly due to changing geopolitical dynamics and concerns about the U.S. economy.

As I have pointed out in a number of previous articles, the U.S. dollar has been declining relative to many foreign currencies. We can see this by simply looking at the U.S. dollar index (DXY) year-to-date:

As we can see, the U.S. dollar index has fallen by 8.81% year-to-date, which has had an impact on American markets. This impact may not be immediately apparent in the eyes of American investors, but to foreigners, it reduces the effective returns that they earn on their assets when those assets are measured in foreign currency terms. After all, for an investor in Europe who is looking to invest euros, a U.S. stock that remains stable in U.S. dollar terms will actually be falling because this hypothetical foreign investor is looking at it in terms of euros. In addition, dividends paid out by American companies will actually be declining for European investors who need to convert the U.S. dollar-denominated dividend payments back into euros in order to spend them. When they make the conversion back, the U.S. dollar payments convert into fewer euros, even if they stayed the same in U.S. dollars. It is, in other words, the exact opposite of what U.S. investors who owned dividend-paying European assets experienced over the past six months.

This could be important for a utility company such as Spire, because utilities deliver a significant proportion of their total return in the form of dividends. For example, take a look at this chart showing Spire’s price return charted against its total return over the past ten years:

As we can see, Spire’s share price only increased by 45.59% over the ten-year period, but investors in the company actually realized a 108.1% total return over the period. This tells us that, clearly, more than half of the total return provided by Spire over the past decade was due to the dividend. Dividends are paid in U.S. dollars, so when the dollar declines against foreign currency, then the future value of the company’s earnings and dividends declines in the eyes of investors that are using that currency. As such, the company’s appeal declines, and foreigners may be less apt to invest money in the company.

Thus, one thing that we have been seeing in the U.S. capital markets over the past six months is, in many ways, the exact opposite situation from what was occurring during the decade or so following the financial crisis. During that period, U.S. stocks surged while most foreign markets saw minimal movement. Over the past ten years, the MSCI All-Countries World ex-US Index (ACWX) is only up 30.58% compared to a 185.10% gain in the S&P 500 Index (SP500):

One reason for this is that foreigners were opting to invest their money in the United States as opposed to their own markets. Over the past six months, this attitude has changed for the reasons that we have just discussed. This could create a drag on U.S. stock performance, although the degree to which an individual stock is affected by a reduction in foreign demand will vary.

Fortunately, Spire’s stock performance has been pretty solid since the last time that we discussed the company. This chart shows how its share price performed against the S&P 500 Index and the domestic utility sector (XLU):

As we can see, Spire’s share price is up a fairly impressive 12.07%, substantially beating the S&P 500 Index’s 1.15% decline and the 7.74% gain in the domestic utility sector. At least on some level, it is not really surprising to see a utility company outperform the S&P 500 Index. First of all, utility companies do not have the same foreign ownership that some of the more well-known technology companies have. For example, the Swiss National Bank achieved a certain amount of notoriety over the past decade for making large purchases of Apple (AAPL), Tesla (TSLA), and a few other mega-cap U.S. stocks. As of March 31, 2022, the Swiss National Bank owned $177 billion worth of U.S.-traded securities. Utilities stocks, in contrast, are somewhat overlooked and not as frequently purchased by foreign investors. As such, a fall in the U.S. dollar would not necessarily have as big an impact on reducing demand for these equities as it would the technology sector.

In addition, we have something of a flight to safety trade occurring with utilities. As I have mentioned in a number of previous articles, utilities are not particularly affected by economic shocks such as recessions or tariffs. For the most part, tariffs do not affect these companies much as they do not import significantly. In addition, they are relatively insulated against recessions because people will typically prioritize paying their utility bills ahead of making discretionary purchases. During the first half of this year, we experienced a market shock due to investors not knowing what to expect following the American imposition of tariffs on most of the rest of the world, as well as fears that the United States would enter into a recession during the second half of 2025. Thus, the environment was ripe for a safe haven investment, and while Treasuries largely failed, utilities did pretty well a safe haven investment.

On a total return basis, Spire did even better since our last discussion:

As we can see, investors in Spire have realized a 13.24% total return since our last discussion, which has been a very good performance given the market conditions that have dominated most of this year. Overall, it seems certain that any investor will likely be very pleased here. Spire’s performance year-to-date shows the advantages of diversification, as the gains that this company has delivered year-to-date could offset losses elsewhere in a portfolio.

About Spire

As stated in the introduction, Spire is primarily a regulated natural gas utility that serves the states of Missouri, Alabama, and Mississippi. The bulk of the company’s operations are in Missouri, though, as it serves the two largest cities in the state (Kansas City and St. Louis) as well as their suburbs. As of March 2025, Spire serves 1,213,310 customers in Missouri compared to 429,596 in Alabama:

Presumably, the company’s customer count in Mississippi is much smaller than this, as it claims to only have approximately 1.7 million customers across the three states. The totals provided above for Missouri and Alabama alone total 1,642,906, or pretty close to the 1.7 million total figure. The company’s utility business that serves parts of Mississippi is a single unit called Spire EnergySouth, which has approximately 100,000 customers. From the most recent 10-Q filing:

Spire Gulf of Spire Mississippi are utilities engaged in the purchase, retail distribution and sale of natural gas to approximately 100,000 customers in southern Alabama and south-central Mississippi. Spire Gulf is regulated by the APSC, and Spire Mississippi is regulated by the MSPSC.

The addition of Spire EnergySouth brings the company’s total customer count up to approximately 1,742,906, which rounds off to 1.7 million. Of these, about 70.59% are located in Missouri, so we can clearly see that this is by far the most important state for the company.

This could pose some difficulty for Spire in terms of demographics. Kansas City is generally considered to be a growing city. According to World Population Review, Kansas City is growing at a relatively meager rate of 0.16% annually. The population is currently estimated to be approximately 512,400, and it is projected to reach 515,700 by the end of 2029:

This is not an especially rapid rate of growth when compared to some of the more rapidly growing cities in the United States, but at least it is some growth.

The challenge for Spire in terms of demographics comes from the city of St. Louis. World Population Review puts the current population of the city at 269,300 people. However, this city’s population is declining at a 2.27% rate. It is expected to fall to 244,300 people by 2029:

This is more than sufficient to offset all of the demographic strength found in Kansas City. As a few simple calculations will reveal, Kansas City’s population is expected to increase by a total of 3,300 people between now and 2029. However, the decline in St. Louis’s population is expected to be 25,000 over the same period. That obviously completely offsets the gain in Kansas City.

Admittedly, this does not necessarily mean that there is not some movement to the suburbs going on. After all, Spire does provide natural gas services to the suburbs of both of these cities as well, and one trend that we have been seeing in the United States in recent years has been a movement of population out of the cities and into the surrounding suburbs. As such, just because the population of the cities themselves is declining in aggregate, investors may not have a reason to panic just yet. Spire did manage to increase its customer base by 9,465 over the past year in the state of Missouri, after all. While the increase in Missouri was partially offset by a decrease of 849 customers in Alabama, overall, the company did manage to grow its customer count over the past year. This naturally means that the company currently has more customers paying their monthly bills than was the case at this time last year.

Data Center Opportunities

In a recent article, I pointed out that the expansion of cloud computing and artificial intelligence might prove to be quite beneficial for natural gas utilities. This seems rather counterintuitive since none of these companies are actually involved in the development of artificial intelligence, and data centers are powered by electricity instead of natural gas. However, it is indeed the electric-hungry nature of artificial intelligence data centers that is beneficial for natural gas utilities. This comes from the fact that it has been proving difficult for companies constructing data centers that are intended to be used for generative artificial intelligence servers, to obtain connections to the electric grid in a timely manner. As MicroGrid Knowledge points out, the average waiting time to connect a data center to the electric grid today is about five years. That is too long for many of these developers, especially considering how rapidly generative artificial intelligence technology is progressing. As such, they have started to opt for putting natural gas generators on-site, which then generate the electricity that they cannot get from the electric grid. Naturally, the owners and operators of the data centers need to obtain the natural gas to drive these generators from somewhere, and natural gas utilities are the most convenient source in many cases.

For its part, Spire makes no mention of data center construction or deployment as a driver of its forward growth in its most recent earnings presentation. The company only provides the following drivers:

However, this does not necessarily mean that there are no growth opportunities for Spire from the provision of natural gas to data centers.

Kansas City is certainly seeing some data center development activity. In mid-December 2024, Edged announced the grand opening of an artificial intelligence data center in Kansas City.

Data Center Map states that there are 46 data centers located in Missouri, nearly all of which are in Kansas City or St. Louis:

These are obviously the two cities to which Spire provides natural gas utility services.

The Missouri Partnership is actively working to attract data centers to the state, and it points out a few factors that developers may be interested in. First of all, Missouri is offering sales tax exemptions to data centers, and has a fairly large base of 161,200 experienced technology workers located in the state. This has been sufficient to attract some of the largest technology companies in the country to construct new data centers in the state. For example, in March 2024, Alphabet (GOOGL) (GOOG) announced that it would be constructing a $1 billion data center in Kansas City. Meta Platforms (META) constructed an $800 million data center in Kansas City back in 2023. Finally, TierPoint purchased a building in St. Louis last year that it intends to convert into a data center.

Admittedly, so far, most of the data centers that have been constructed in Missouri in recent years have relied on the electric grid to obtain their power. Spire does not operate any electric distribution infrastructure, so it has been unable to take advantage of this. However, given the trend of data center operators wishing to have electric generation on-site powered by natural gas, it seems like Spire could be able to take advantage of this should any developers come to Missouri that include on-site generation in their development plan. Given the state’s apparent efforts to attract developers, that is a very real possibility.

Other Growth Opportunities

With that said, the possibility of data center deployment and demand for natural gas is not the only potential source of growth for Spire. The company recently stated that it has the intention of investing $840 million into its infrastructure over the full-year 2025 period. This money will be split between improving the safety and reliability of its current infrastructure and expanding its infrastructure to service new customers:

While the company’s spending on improvements to the safety and reliability of its infrastructure will dwarf the spending on expanding its system, it is not the case that only a minority of the spending will provide a return and growth. On the contrary, all of this spending will directly contribute to either offsetting the depreciation of the company’s assets in the current year or increasing the size of the company’s rate base. The company expects that this spending will increase its rate base by about 7% to 8% in Missouri, and about 6% in Alabama and Mississippi. Whenever we take into account that the company will have to issue new equity to fund this capital spending program, we arrive at an approximate earnings-per-share growth rate of 5% to 7%.

If the company’s earnings multiples remain the same, then we should expect its stock price to increase at an average rate of about 5% to 7% per year, which is slightly higher than the 45.59% capital appreciation that Spire has delivered in total over the past decade. However, the company also has a 4.17% dividend yield at the current price, and it has increased the dividend in each of the past 22 years. There is no reason to expect that it will not deliver a similar dividend increase in 2025. This is especially likely to be the case if the company’s earnings per share increases along with its rate base. When we take this into account, investors should be able to expect a total return of about 9% to 11% on average going forward. This is obviously nothing amazing, but it is not really that bad for a safe-haven utility stock.

Valuation

According to Zacks Investment Research, Spire will grow its earnings per share at a 6.54% rate over the next three to five years. This is in line with the earnings per share growth projections that came about from the company’s expected rate base growth and financing needs, so it seems like it should be pretty solid. This earnings-per-share growth rate puts the company’s price-to-earnings growth ratio at 2.56, given the current stock price.

Here is how Spire’s valuation compares with its peers in this respect:

This metric states that Spire is fairly reasonably valued against its peers. The company is not the most expensive one shown. However, it is not nearly as cheap as a company such as Southwest Gas Holdings.

On the other hand, Seeking Alpha’s Quant system suggests that Spire is quite reasonably priced right now. As we can see here, the system has assigned the company a valuation grade of B:

For the most part, the company looks better than the sector median in all categories. It is generally cheaper than the utilities sector in terms of price-to-earnings, enterprise value, price-to-book, and price-to-cash flow. In addition, the company’s dividend yield is a bit higher than the utility sector median, which might attract some investors who are seeking both income and value.

For the most part, Spire looks quite good here. The company’s valuation grade is more attractive than most of its peers, and it has somewhat superior growth potential when compared against Northwest Natural Holding (the only company that beat it in terms of Quant valuation).

Overall, there could be a case made for buying Spire at the current level. It certainly does not appear to be excessively expensive, at least.

Conclusion

In conclusion, Spire is a regulated natural gas utility that serves the states of Missouri, Alabama, and Mississippi. Natural gas utilities are somewhat less popular among investors than electric utilities, which is at least partially due to the expectations that electric utilities will experience greater demand growth than natural gas utilities. However, as we have started to see, natural gas should not be completely discounted as it may play a very big role in onsite electric generation for data centers, and it is cheaper than electricity for space heating. The general perception of natural gas, though, has made companies like Spire somewhat more attractively priced than electric utilities, and they have higher yields.

For its part, Spire looks quite reasonable at the current price, and it has substantially outperformed the market year-to-date. The company could be a safe haven in times of uncertainty, and investors might want to consider holding some of its shares to reduce volatility.
