data.title: CrowdStrike Q1 Earnings: Why I'm Not Impressed
data.description:
I recently discussed CrowdStrike Holdings Inc. (CRWD), downgrading the stock to a hold due to overvaluation, extended technical conditions, and a general feel of hype surrounding the critical cybersecurity company. Now, this hold rating followed a buy rating I had on CrowdStrike following its outage meltdown and the 2024 summer correction.

CRWD Stock 1-Year Chart

CrowdStrike's stock dropped precipitously last summer, creating an excellent buying opportunity. However, given CrowdStrike's recent lackluster earnings, ultra-high valuation, competition concerns, and other variables, CrowdStrike remains a hold here, in my view.

CrowdStrike: High In The Sky Expensive Here

Investors had very high expectations for CrowdStrike, especially after BTIG reported that the recent cybersecurity channels check surrounding CrowdStrike was largely positive. The analyst also bumped his price target up from $431 to $520.

You know, it's interesting because a $520 price tag would put CrowdStrike's market cap at about $140 billion. This dynamic illustrates that CrowdStrike would trade at about 30 times this year's projected sales estimates, which is extremely expensive.

Yes, I can think of one big cap company that is more expensive, Palantir (PLTR), trading at about 80 times sales and a 230 P/E multiple. However, Palantir is essentially a monopoly, and CrowdStrike is not.

Also, Palantir may be incredibly overvalued. So, it's not saying much when comparing Palantir to other stocks. I've just seen this comparison made several times now, which is why I am bringing it up here.

The Numbers Don't Lie

CrowdStrike could deliver about $3.50 in EPS this year, which is about a 10% YoY decline, and places CrowdStrike's P/E ratio at a nose bleeding ultra-high ratio of about 130.

Even if we look out to next year, CrowdStrike could deliver about $4.75 in EPS. This dynamic implies that CrowdStrike's forward P/E ratio is around 100, still very high and there is uncertainty surrounding earnings.

The Market Expected Better

Again, expectations for CrowdStrike were sky-high, and the stock was priced for perfection. Unfortunately, we witnessed little perfection in CrowdStrike's earnings numbers. Yes, the company reported a non-GAAP EPS beat as it delivered $0.73 in EPS vs. the consensus estimate for $0.66.

However, much of the good news stopped right there, as CrowdStrike reported weaker than expected revenues, which came in at $1.1 billion, a $10 million miss. While the miss seems minor, it becomes a problem when a stock is priced for perfection. We want to see beats, and missing on sales just isn't going to cut it for many investors here, in my view.

The Forward Guidance Was Just Okay

So, we really want to see better than expected guidance, especially when a stock is trading at such incredibly high valuations. Unfortunately, CrowdStrike's Q2 and full-year guidance was just okay, but nothing special. The guidance may not support such an uber-high valuation for CrowdStrike's stock, in my view.

Q2 sales guidance was actually below consensus. CrowdStrike expects Q2 sales to be in the range of $1.145-1.512B. This is below the consensus $1.16B estimate. The full-year guide was for sales of $4.743-4.805B vs. the estimate for $4.79B.

These are not good guidance numbers. In fact, we can deduce that CrowdStrike may even miss the $4.79B full year sales estimate. Therefore, we see that CrowdStrike is struggling to some extent, and it's challenging to justify the ultra-high multiples, especially when CrowdStrike can't even keep up with the current potentially lowballed estimates.

Moving Forward

I don't expect CRWD stock to crater here, but we may see a more considerable pullback. CrowdStrike is pulling back from its ATH of nearly $500, and we may see about a 10-20% pullback process. CrowdStrike's $400/50-day MA support seems like a solid support level, and I would consider repurchasing my CrowdStrike shares around there.

It's possible to see a more substantial pullback, but this may only materialize if the broader market corrects by 5-10%. In a worse case outcome, CrowdStrike could potentially correct to about $350, but the downside should be limited beyond there.

The good news for CrowdStrike is that despite its terrible outage event, CrowdStrike remains the top cybersecurity stock globally. Yes, it has competition from SentinelOne (S), Zscaler (ZS), Palo Alto Networks (PANW), and others.

Nonetheless, CrowdStrike is the market leader, arguably providing the best "whole package," when it comes down to satisfying a client's cybersecurity needs. Therefore, its valuation is subjective, and while it appears incredibly rich, the market is willing to pay top dollar for it. Still, we need the growth story to match up to the valuation, and the company must execute better to keep the market interested in its stock at current lofty valuations.

data.title: Euro Trades Quietly Ahead Of The ECB's Rate Cut
data.description:
Overview

The dollar is mostly softer today in narrow trading ranges, though it is firmer against the yen and Swiss franc. The weakness of US data (ADP, ISM services, and anecdotal Beige Book) lifted the market confidence of at least two Fed cuts this year. Most emerging market currencies are also trading higher against the dollar today. Still, overall, the foreign exchange market is relatively calm, and ranges are mostly narrow. The outcome of the ECB meeting today (rate cut and reductions in this year's growth and inflation forecasts are likely) and tomorrow's US employment report are awaited.

Equities are generally firmer. Among the large markets in the Asia Pacific region, Japan and Australia did not participate in today's advance. Europe's Stoxx 600 (STOXX) is rising for the third consecutive session. US index futures are firm. Bond markets are also bid. Japan's 30-year bond auction was alright. The bid-cover of 2.92 though is not regarded as inspiring. Still, the 10-year yield fell almost four basis points to 1.45%. European benchmark yields are mostly 3-4 bps lower. The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) is slightly softer near 4.34%. Gold is firm in the upper end of this week's range, which is a little below $3400. July WTI is trading quietly in a $62.50-$63.15 range. It has been capped this week below $64. Last month's high was closer to $64.20.

USD: The dollar traded poorly yesterday. Weighed down by a surprisingly poor ADP private sector jobs estimate (37k vs. 114k expected), a softer than expected ISM services (the first sub-50 reading since June 2024), and a Beige Book that underscored drag of the economic and policy uncertainty, the Dollar Index trended lower in North America. Tuesday's low was slightly below 98.60 and this was approached yesterday. The more important level is 97.90-98.00, the three-year low set last month. It is trading quietly today in a narrow range below 99.00. We already know that the US April goods deficit narrowed dramatically to $87.6 bln from the rush to get ahead of the tariffs that ballooned the deficit to $162.3 bln in March. Goods imports in April tumbled by almost 20%, a record. Exports rose 3.4%. The overall April trade balance will be reported today and is seen narrowing to around $66 bln from $140.50 bln. The US reports Q1 productivity and unit labor costs. These are among the economic phenomena that are not observed directly but deduced from the GDP data. Productivity contracted (-0.8%) after growing by an average of 2.1% in 2024 and 3.1% in 2023. Unit labor costs are a mirror image. They rose by an average of slightly less than 2% last year and a little more than 2% in 2023. They jumped 5.7% in Q1 25, pending today's revision. Weekly jobless claims may draw some attention after last week's report (week through May 23) showed a 14k job to 240k, the first increase in four weeks, and the second highest since early last October. Still, the national jobs report tomorrow is more important. Slower nonfarm payroll growth is expected (~130k vs 177k) but the key may be the unemployment rate. It was at 4.2% in March and April, matching last year's high. The Atlanta Fed's GDP tracker will be updated later today. On Monday, it was raised to 4.6% from 3.8%.

Euro: The euro settled firmly yesterday and above $1.14. Options for 2.4 bln euros struck there expire today. It is holding above $1.1400 so far today and has been unable to rise above $1.1435. A move above $1.1455 re-targets the 3 1/2-year high set last month near $1.1575. Above there, the initial scope may extend another cent. There is practically no doubt that the ECB will deliver another quarter-point cut today. The sub-2% aggregate May CPI removes whatever lingering doubt there may have been. There seems to be two issues. First are the updated forecasts. It is reasonable to expect growth and inflation forecasts to be trimmed. In March, the ECB saw the economy expanding by 0.9% this year, 1.2% next, and 1.3% in 2027. It had CPI at 2.3% this year, 1.9% in 2026, and 2.0% in 2027. For comparison purposes, note that the IMF's updated forecasts are for 0.8% growth this year and shares the ECB's outlook for the next two years. Its CPI projection is 2.1% this year and again converges with the ECB for 2026 and 2027. The second issue is forward guidance. We suspect ECB President Lagarde to be circumspect and non-committal. Barring a new shock, a pause seems to be in order. So, no cut in July, while September, which sees new forecasts, may be a closer call.

CNY: The greenback's broad weakness saw it fall for the second consecutive session against the Chinese yuan. The dollar was sold to an eight-day low near CNH7.1700. It is holding today, and the dollar has pushed up to about CNH7.1820. Last week's low, the lowest since last November, was near CNH7.1615. The PBOC lowered the dollar's reference rate after increasing it for the past two sessions (CNY7.1865 vs. CNY7.1886). Caixin's services PMI ticked up to 51.1 from 50.7 but the much weaker than expected manufacturing PMI (48.3 vs 50.4 in April) was sufficient to drag the composite PMI lower to 49.6 from 51.1. It is the first break of 50 since the end of 2022. The headwinds from the trade dispute with the US appear to be growing again as container shipment to the US have fallen again. The facture of the agreement struck in Geneva seems to be over chips and critical minerals and magnets. Yet, it seems that China is progressing with its largely domestic chip industry, which is being forced to innovate somewhat differently. At the same time, the replacement of China's capacity in critical minerals (and their processing) and magnets seems more challenging for the US to replace in the short- to medium-term.

JPY: The dollar initially rose to a three-day-high yesterday near JPY144.40, but the softer US data and the 10 bp pullback in the US 10-year yield provided too much, even if the correlation has slackened lately. The dollar was sold to almost JPY142.60. It made a marginal new low today near JPY142.50 before recovering to JPY143.40. Tuesday's low was closer to JPY142.40. The JPY142 area offers support. Labor cash earnings were steady at 2.3% year-over-year in April. Consider that in April 2024, they had risen by 1.6% year-over-year. Yet, adjusted for inflation, real cash earnings continue to fall. They fell 1.8% in the year through April. In April 2024, they had fallen by 1.2% on a year-over-year basis, and in April 2023, they were off 3.2%. This is likely one of the factors that constrain household spending, which will be reported tomorrow. It is seen slowing to 1.5% in April from 2.1% in March.

GBP: Sterling rose to $1.3580 on the back of the weaker dollar. It is trading in a narrow range slightly below there today (~GBP1.3540-$1.3575). The high in late May was near $1.3595. Above $1.3600, the next highs from March 2022 come into view near $1.3645. The upper Bollinger Band is slightly above there today. The 2022 high was recorded in January (~$1.3750). The UK reported a small increase in auto registrations (a proxy for auto sales) and the third consecutive rise in the construction PMI, which remains below 50 as it has since the end of last year.

CAD: The Bank of Canada held policy steady yesterday, but it seems clear that the easing cycle is not over. Still, in the face of the greenback's heavier today, the Canadian dollar rose to its best level since last October. The US dollar approached CAD1.3650. It has held today but looks vulnerable as the upside has been capped near CAD1.3685. The lower Bollinger Band is near CAD1.3625 today. From a technical perspective, there is little to hang one's hat on until closer to CAD1.3600, and even then, the "support" does not look particularly strong. Canada reports its April merchandise trade balance today. Although it reported a nearly C$2 bln trade deficit in February and March combined, the nearly $3.1 bln surplus in January offset it in full. The means in Q1 25, Canada recorded slightly more than a C$1 bln goods surplus. In Q1 24, a small deficit was reported. Canada's IVEY PMI is also due. It typically is more volatile and runs hotter than the PMI composite. It was at 47.9 in April, down from 51.30 in March, which was the first back-to-back decline since July-August 2024.

AUD: The Australian dollar pushed above $0.6500 in North America yesterday and again failed to close above it. It is trying again today and is bid around $0.6515 in late European morning turnover. Last week's high was slightly above $0.6535, a little shy of the $0.6550 area we targeted, which corresponds to the (61.8%) retracement of the losses from last October (~$0.6940) to the April low (~$0.5915). Australia reported its goods trade surplus earlier today. It narrowed from March's A$6.9 bln, which was the largest since January 2024 to A$5.4 bln. That is still above last year's average (~A$5.7 bln). Exports slowed. They slipped by about 2.4% after jumping 7.2% in March (the largest gain since April 2022). Imports rose by 1.1% after tumbling 2.4% in March. Separately, household spending rose by 0.1% in April after falling 0.3% in March. At an annualized rate, it is rising at a 2.4% pace. In the first four months of 2024, household spending rose at an annualized rate of around 3.3%.

MXN: The weaker greenback, lower US rates, and equity gains helped underpin the peso yesterday. The peso rose to its best level since last October. The US dollar approached MXN19.1625 before stabilizing. It is in a narrow range between MXN19.1895 and MXN19.2135. It has been a slow but steady grind lower from May's high set on May 6 near MXN19.7820. We continue to see potential toward the MXN19.00 area initially. Yesterday, Mexico reported a dramatic 10.8% surge in May auto sales. The reality may not be as impressive. The report is not seasonally adjusted, and auto sales have risen every May beginning in 2006. It was the sixth time that May auto sales rose by more than 10%. May auto sales were about 0.4% lower than sales in May 2024. Separately, Brazil's composite PMI slipped lower and remained under 50 for the second consecutive month. Disappointing real sector data and the recent softer PMI print may give the market reason to re-think the likelihood of a rate hike at the June 18 central bank meeting. The dollar approached BRL5.61 yesterday, with BRL5.60 offering important support. It has been frayed on an intraday basis a few times here in Q2 but has not settled below it once.

Original Post

data.title: My Latest Buy Yields 21%: ECC
data.description:
Co-authored by Treading Softly

Imagine having the cash on hand to buy whatever you want whenever you want.

For some, this is a pipe dream that they'll never achieve, but only because they limit their ability to gain income from the market. I don't believe that most of us will ever have the ability to frivolously spend money at completely absent-minded paces and not suffer some form of harm. Yet for most of us, we could unlock the ability to have more money than we need to live off, and then put that excess into a savings account, or reinvest it back into the market. This is a key aspect of being an income investor and a crucial component of our Income Method – to unlock substantial income streams from the market and reinvest them back into our portfolio, allowing it to grow continuously. One way to do this is by investing in or buying what I like to call "income catalysts." These are investment opportunities that have massive yields, often because they’re misunderstood, that allow you to collect massive sums of income today.

What if I told you that you can get this massive income from a company that is fully covering the distribution going out the door? It sounds too perfect, right? Let's take a look.

Gobs of Cash Flow

Eagle Point Credit (NYSE:ECC), yielding 21.6%, is a CEF (Closed-End Fund) that primarily invests in CLO equity tranches, which we've covered previously. ECC diversifies more than Oxford Lane Capital (OXLC), but with 79% of its portfolio invested in CLO equity, that will be the main driver of earnings and NAV movements. Source

As management described a few quarters ago, the main attraction to CLO equity is that it produces "gobs" of cash flow. Sure enough, ECC has had another quarter where it produced gobs of cash flow. At $0.69/share, that was enough to cover the dividend, pay all the expenses, and still have $0.08/share excess:

Rarely does something with a 20%+ yield cover its distribution so easily.

Let's take a look at a few metrics in ECC's portfolio. Here is a look at ECC's portfolio characteristics.

Reinvestment Period

ECC's focus over the past few years has been extending the reinvestment period for the CLOs it holds. In Q1, the period has been extended to 3.5 years, compared to 2.5 years in Q1 2024. The reinvestment period is a positive for equity investors because it means that when a loan prepays, the principal is reinvested. When the reinvestment period expires, the principal is used to retire the senior tranches, causing the CLO to deleverage and with less leverage comes less cash flow. Reinvestment is particularly appealing when loan prices are trading below par. ECC's portfolio has an average loan price of 96.66% of par. If $100 in par value is repaid, the CLO gets $100 even if the market value is $96.66, during the reinvestment period the CLO can take that $100 and buy a replacement loan. At an average price of 96.66% of par, $100 can purchase $103.45 in par value for the new loan.

This is powerful for the CLO equity because the principal that has to be repaid to the debt tranches is fixed. So an extra $3.45 in par value is an extra $3.45 that will go to the CLO equity at the end of the CLO lifespan. This provides potential for capital gains and helps offset realized credit losses. The longer the reinvestment period is, the more time the manager has to take advantage of these opportunities.

Average Junior OC Cushion

The junior OC (overcollateralization) cushion is a measure of how many defaults can be absorbed before the CLO fails its first collateralization test and funds start being redirected. Redirection is designed to protect the senior tranches. If a collateralization test is failed, any cash coming into the CLO is used to repay debt tranches until the debt is low enough that the test is passed. This means that cash flow for the equity tranche is paused. We saw this happen during COVID, and it was the main driver of distributions being reduced throughout the sector.

So for ECC, the higher the Junior OC Cushion is, the better. At 4.61%, this indicates that ECC's average CLO could absorb a default rate of 4.61% before safeguards take effect. In Q4 2019, this measure was 3.86%. The current trailing 12-month default rate for leveraged loans is 0.8%.

The risk of a shock and a sudden spike in defaults is always present, but the cushion is growing, which means that a shock will be more easily absorbed today than it was during the COVID-19 pandemic.

Currency: USD Exposure

ECC is a fund that has proven itself willing to take advantage of any opportunities management identifies. Over the past two quarters, it has become apparent that management sees an opportunity outside of the US Dollar. USD investments now account for 90% of the portfolio, while non-USD investments are nearly 10%. A year ago, ECC was well over 99% USD.

While the U.S. remains the dominant portion of its portfolio, this shift over the past two quarters has likely become substantial enough that it could generate a variation from peers that haven't ventured into European CLOs. Looking through ECC's portfolio detail in their supplement, we can see why they found European CLOs attractive, as the spreads they are paying on the senior debt are lower than average, while the spreads they are receiving from the loans held by the CLO are higher than average. Lower income expenses and higher interest income are a great combination.

Conclusion

It's an interesting period for CLOs because there is certainly a perception that the economy is weakening, and the risk of defaults rising in a weak economy is there. On the other hand, realized default rates have been historically low, even as loan prices have been below par for several years now. Usually, lower loan prices come with defaults. An extended period of below-par loan prices has allowed CLOs to build par and improve their Junior OC cushions so that if a default wave does come, it will be more easily absorbed.

This creates an unusual combination where prices are lower, yields are higher, but risk is more contained than it has been during historical periods of disruption in the credit markets. Leveraged loans are trading at prices they were trading at in May 2020:

In May 2020, defaults were actively occurring, and many were fearing much worse. For CLOs, funds were being redirected, and cash flow was being reduced, while there were serious questions about how much capital would be lost permanently.

Today, defaults are happening at a pace substantially below the historical average and near all-time lows. There is no particular reason to believe defaults will skyrocket soon, as borrowers are operating at relatively modest leverage. Companies deleveraged during the COVID-19 pandemic, and many haven't leveraged back up as interest rates skyrocketed.

In short, the CLO market is priced at the same level it was after the Black Swan already happened, while the defaults were actively happening, and when cash flow was already significantly reduced.

When the market is selling gobs of cash flow this cheap, I simply say "Thank you."
